reasoning: The recursive call at lines 116-118 (if (is.existy(req.raw)) { return getClientIp(req.raw); }) is confirmed present with no depth guard or cycle detection. is.existy only checks value != null, so a circular reference req.raw === req would cause unlimited recursion and a stack overflow. However, the reachability claim is critically flawed. The req parameter is the HTTP request object constructed by the framework (Express, Fastify, etc.). HTTP attackers control headers, body, and URL — not JavaScript object properties like .raw. No HTTP input path sets req.raw to point back to req. In Fastify, req.raw is the underlying Node IncomingMessage (req.raw !== req under normal operation). To trigger this, an attacker must be able to assign a JavaScript property on the req object inside the same process, which requires either: (1) a developer bug creating an accidental circular reference, or (2) malicious code running in the same Node process. There is no schema validation (zod/joi/ajv) involved because the input is an object reference, not user-supplied data. The vulnerability is not reachable via external HTTP requests. The finding conflates a theoretical code quality concern (missing cycle guard) with an exploitable network-level DoS. The code pattern exists but the described attack vector does not apply to real deployments under a normal HTTP threat model.
